player_analysis.inferred_strategy: Central banks are building to ensure they control the settlement layer for high-value sovereign corridors. mBridge specifically targets China-Middle East-Southeast Asia corridors where geopolitical alignment makes bilateral CBDC settlement rational. BIS knows it cannot coordinate 190+ countries on a single CBDC system; instead it's building frameworks (Nexus) that connect heterogeneous national systems. This creates space for private infrastructure in corridors that are too small, too numerous, or too politically fragmented for sovereign coordination.

behavioral_patterns.pattern: Institutional players are building settlement infrastructure on parallel tracks without coordinating — each major actor (BIS, DTCC, Canton/Goldman, Ripple, Visa/Stripe) is building independently for their specific use case

Resource commitment follows use-case segmentation: central banks invest in sovereign corridors, banks invest in securities settlement, payment companies invest in retail stablecoin rails, Ripple invests in cross-border institutional payment infrastructure. No single player is capturing the entire settlement stack.

Players with the strongest say-do alignment (Visa/Stripe, BIS, SBI) are the ones committing the most capital. Players with larger say-do gaps (DTCC, SWIFT) are primarily positioning rhetorically. This suggests the competitive landscape is being shaped by deployers, not announcers.

Opacity increases as competitive pressure intensifies. Ripple stops publishing ODL data at the exact moment competitors are deploying at scale. This pattern is consistent with a player who knows their current metrics aren't competitive with the narrative they've built.

Regulatory capture vs regulatory avoidance: Dimon pushes for bank-level stablecoin regulation (protects incumbent banks), BIS builds sovereign-only systems (protects central banks), Ripple/Circle seek lighter-touch crypto regulation (enables growth). Each player's regulatory stance perfectly predicts their competitive strategy.

The platform expansion to fiat, custody, and virtual accounts further supports this — they're building revenue streams that don't depend on XRP settlement volume, which is exactly what you'd do if settlement volume growth was disappointing.
